title: Isaiah 65
subtitle: New Heavens and a New Earth

sections.header: Verses 1–16 — “I Revealed Myself to Those Who Did Not Ask for Me”

heb.paragraph: "I revealed myself to those who did not ask (dārash) for me; I was found by those who did not seek me. To a nation that did not call on my name, I said, ‘Here am I, here am I’" (v.1). Paul quotes this in Romans 10:20 for Gentile inclusion. God spread out his hands all day to an obstinate people (v.2). They provoke him with pagan practices: gardens, incense on bricks, graves at night, pig flesh (vv.3–4). "Stand by yourself; don’t come near me, for I am too sacred for you!" (v.5) — arrogant holiness. God will repay (v.6). But a remnant is preserved: "As when juice is still found in a cluster of grapes and people say, ‘Don’t destroy it, there is still a blessing in it’" (v.8). "My servants will eat, but you will go hungry. My servants will rejoice, but you will be put to shame" (v.13). The separation of faithful and unfaithful within Israel.

notes.note: "I revealed myself to those who did not ask for me." Romans 10:20: Paul applies this to the Gentiles. God takes the initiative toward those who never sought him.
"Don’t destroy it, there is still a blessing in it." The grape-cluster remnant: even in corrupt Israel, juice remains. The remnant theology in agricultural form.

"My servants will eat, you will go hungry. My servants will drink, you will go thirsty." The binary: servants feast, rebels starve. "Whoever invokes a blessing will do so by the God of truth." The new name: God of truth (ʾamen).

refs.note: Paul quotes Isaiah's contrast between God revealing himself to those who didn't seek and his patience with disobedient Israel.
Paul applies the pattern of calling those who were not my people 'my people' to Gentile inclusion.
Paul and Barnabas's turn to Gentiles cites Isaiah to justify mission beyond Israel.

hist.context: The opening reversal — God found by those who didn't seek him — addresses the paradox of Gentile responsiveness versus Israelite resistance. The syncretistic practices described (gardens, secret places, eating pig flesh) reflect persistent idolatry in the post-exilic community. The 'servants' who receive blessing versus those who forsake God establishes the remnant concept central to Isaiah's theology.

sections.header: Verses 17–25 — “I Will Create New Heavens and a New Earth”

heb.paragraph: "See, I will create (bārāʾ) new heavens and a new earth. The former things will not be remembered, nor will they come to mind" (v.17). Revelation 21:1: "Then I saw a new heaven and a new earth" — drawn directly from this verse. "I will create Jerusalem to be a delight and its people a joy" (v.18). "Never again will there be an infant who lives but a few days" (v.20). "They will build houses and dwell in them; they will plant vineyards and eat their fruit" (v.21). No more stolen labour: "they will not build and others inhabit; they will not plant and others eat" (v.22). "Before they call I will answer; while they are still speaking I will hear" (v.24). "The wolf and the lamb will feed together. They will neither harm nor destroy on all my holy mountain" (v.25) — the 11:6–9 vision returns. The answer to 64:12: God will not hold back. He will create everything new.

notes.note: "I will create new heavens and a new earth." bārāʾ — the creation-verb of Genesis 1:1. Not renovation but new creation. Rev 21:1 quotes this. The former things: forgotten.
"No infant who lives but a few days. They will build houses and dwell in them." The new creation is concrete: long life, secure homes, no stolen labour. Not abstract heaven but renewed earth.
"Before they call I will answer." The ultimate intimacy: God responds before the prayer is spoken. The gap between need and supply is eliminated.

"The wolf and the lamb will feed together." The 11:6–9 peace-kingdom returns. The predator-prey reconciliation. "They will neither harm nor destroy on all my holy mountain." The last echo of the holy mountain vision.

refs.note: Peter's hope for new heavens and new earth where righteousness dwells directly references Isaiah's vision.
John's new heaven and earth with no more death or crying completes Isaiah's promise.
God wiping away every tear echoes Isaiah's vision of joy replacing weeping.

hist.context: The new heavens and new earth represent the most expansive eschatological vision in Isaiah. Former things forgotten, Jerusalem a delight, lifespans extending like trees, wolf and lamb feeding together — these images shaped Jewish and Christian apocalypticism. The reference to 'my holy mountain' (Zion) maintains continuity between present worship and cosmic transformation.

rec.text: ""I revealed myself to those who did not ask for me." Romans 10:20: Paul applies this to the Gentiles. God takes the initiative toward those who never sought him."

rows.label: Revealed to the Unseeking — 65:1–16
rows.text: "I revealed myself to those who did NOT ASK" (Rom 10:20). Grape-cluster remnant. Servants feast; rebels starve.

hebtext.note: "I revealed myself to those who did not ask (dārash) for me; I was found by those who did not seek me. To a nation that did not call on my name, I said, ‘Here am I, here am I’" (v.1). Paul quotes this in Romans 10:20 for Gentile inclusion. God spread out his hands all day to an obstinate people (v.2). They provoke him with pagan practices: gardens, incense on bricks, graves at night, pig flesh (vv.3–4). "Stand by yourself; don’t come near me, for I am too sacred for you!" (v.5) — arrogant holiness. God will repay (v.6). But a remnant is preserved: "As when juice is still found in a cluster of grapes and people say, ‘Don’t destroy it, there is still a blessing in it’" (v.8). "My servants will eat, but you will go hungry. My servants will rejoice, but you will be put to shame" (v.13). The separation of faithful and unfaithful within Israel.

"See, I will create (bārāʾ) new heavens and a new earth. The former things will not be remembered, nor will they come to mind" (v.17). Revelation 21:1: "Then I saw a new heaven and a new earth" — drawn directly from this verse. "I will create Jerusalem to be a delight and its people a joy" (v.18). "Never again will there be an infant who lives but a few days" (v.20). "They will build houses and dwell in them; they will plant vineyards and eat their fruit" (v.21). No more stolen labour: "they will not build and others inhabit; they will not plant and others eat" (v.22). "Before they call I will answer; while they are still speaking I will hear" (v.24). "The wolf and the lamb will feed together. They will neither harm nor destroy on all my holy mountain" (v.25) — the 11:6–9 vision returns. The answer to 64:12: God will not hold back. He will create everything new.

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 10: new heavens and new earth (Rev 21:1). Rom 10:20. Wolf and lamb. Before they call, I answer. Sovereignty at 9: God creates entirely new. Mercy at 9: revealed to the unseeking.

coaching.tip: 'I revealed myself to those who did not ask for me; I was found by those who did not seek me' (v. 1). Paul quotes this in Romans 10:20 to explain Gentile inclusion — God reached out to people who weren't looking for him. Isaiah's universalism isn't sentimental; it's aggressive. God doesn't wait to be sought. He initiates contact with the unwilling. Grace precedes seeking.

'I will create new heavens and a new earth' (v. 17). This is the first explicit articulation of what becomes the Bible's final vision — 2 Peter 3:13 and Revelation 21:1 both quote it directly. Isaiah doesn't envision an escape from the material world but its total renovation. The new creation includes long life, housing, agriculture, and peace among animals (v. 25). Heaven comes to earth; earth doesn't evacuate to heaven.
